title.rendered: The Art and Science of Design-Build

content.rendered:
How BIM and VDC Transform Custom Homes

In the world of custom residential construction, the process of transforming a vision into reality is a complex one. It requires a careful balance between artistic design and the scientific precision of construction documents. As architects, builders, and clients strive to bring a common idea to life, challenges often arise when the creative vision and technical requirements don’t align seamlessly. That’s where a Building Information Modeling (BIM) and Virtual Design and Construction (VDC) where firms like Craydl plays pivotal roles in harmonizing these elements and facilitating collaboration among all stakeholders. By acting as the bridge between the art of design and the science of construction, VDC helps ensure better outcomes for both builders and clients.

The Art of Design: An Architect’s Vision for Custom Homes

Every great building starts with an idea – an architect’s vision. Architects are the creative forces behind residential design, crafting spaces that are aesthetically pleasing, functional, and in tune with the client’s needs & surrounding environments. However, this artistic vision needs to be translated into detailed construction plans that meet building codes, structural integrity requirements, and functionality.

The architect’s beautiful design often needs adjustments to become reality—particularly when it comes to systems designs and working with contractors who need clear, precise specifications. Without the right tools, translating an architect’s creative vision into a well-structured set of construction documents can result in misinterpretation, errors, or costly delays. Recent research estimates that there is 28% waste in the average custom construction project!

The Science of Construction: Precision Through Documentation

On the flip side, the science of construction requires rigorous planning and precise documentation. Construction documents serve as the blueprint that guides every phase of the project, from initial excavation to the final finishes. These documents ensure that every contractor, engineer, and subcontractor understands the scope of the project and the specifics of materials, methods, and systems to be used.

However, the process of producing these documents often leaves little room for flexibility or change once they’re created. In traditional methods, miscommunication between the architect, builder, and client can lead to costly mistakes, delays, and even disputes. The result is often a gap between the beautiful design envisioned and the concrete execution of that design, leaving the homeowner and builder to figure it out.

The Solution: BIM and VDC as the Bridge

This is where a BIM/VDC comes in, facilitating the marriage between the art of architecture and the science of construction documents. BIM and VDC represent cutting-edge technologies that use 3D modeling, real-time data analysis, and collaborative workflows to streamline communication and improve accuracy.

1. BIM as the Collaborative Platform:

BIM creates a dynamic, visual representation of the building, allowing architects, builders, and engineers to collaborate in a shared digital space. Craydl specializes in converting raw design concepts into detailed, accurate 3D models that encompass everything from structural elements to interior finishes and MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) systems. This shared model becomes the single source of truth for all stakeholders, eliminating silos and enabling seamless communication.

With BIM, architects can see how their designs will translate into reality and make real-time adjustments based on input from builders and engineers. It allows them to visualize how materials, systems, and design elements interact, facilitating more informed decision-making.

2. VDC for Efficient Project Management:

VDC, an extension of BIM, is a process that integrates 3D models with project scheduling, cost management, and other essential project data. At Craydl, we leverage VDC to create detailed construction simulations, allowing builders to plan every step of the project with pinpoint precision. From determining the sequence of construction tasks to identifying potential challenges before they arise, VDC ensures that the process runs smoothly, minimizing disruptions and unexpected costs.

By combining BIM and VDC, Craydl enables all project stakeholders to see the bigger picture and work collaboratively toward achieving the same goal: bringing the architect’s vision to life while adhering to the technical requirements of the construction process.

Collaboration in the Design-Build Process with BIM and VDC

The true power of BIM and VDC lies in their ability to foster collaboration among all project stakeholders. Whether it’s the architect, builder, engineer, or interior designer, BIM and VDC provide a shared platform where everyone can input, review, and adjust their work in real time.

1. Early Collaboration:

At the start of the project, BIM helps architects and builders collaborate early in the design process. With a 3D model in hand, builders can assess the feasibility of the design, suggest cost-effective alternatives, and address any potential challenges. This early collaboration ensures that the design is not only beautiful but also practical and buildable.

2. Streamlined Documentation and Clash Detection:

As the design progresses, BIM allows for more precise documentation and real-time clash detection. Builders can instantly see if systems will interfere with one another and resolve any conflicts before construction begins. This proactive approach avoids costly change orders and delays during the build phase, ensuring that the project stays on track and on budget.

3. Post-Construction Value:

Once the project is completed, BIM serves as a valuable resource for facility management. The model contains detailed information about every aspect of the home, from structural elements to finishes, allowing future owners or managers to maintain and manage the property efficiently.

Better Outcomes for the Builder and Client

By facilitating communication and collaboration throughout the construction process, Craydl’s BIM and VDC services lead to better outcomes for both the builder and the client. Builders benefit from accurate, up-to-date information that minimizes errors, reduces costs, and ensures timely project completion. Clients, in turn, receive a finished home that aligns closely with their vision, meets their needs, and exceeds their expectations.

Moreover, by integrating interior design into the BIM model, Craydl ensures that every detail of the home, from layout to materials, value engineering, sunlight analysis, cut fill optimization, budget tracking and more are considered and accounted for. This level of detail enhances the client’s experience, providing a clear understanding of how their home will look, feel, and function before construction even begins.

The Net-Net: Balancing Art and Science in Custom Home Design

In the world of custom residential construction, the balance between the art of design and the science of construction is essential. BIM and VDC play a crucial role in facilitating this balance, ensuring that architects, builders, and clients can collaborate seamlessly to achieve better outcomes. By leveraging cutting-edge technologies, we help bring architectural visions to life with precision and efficiency, creating a construction process that is smoother, more transparent, and ultimately more successful for everyone involved.

title.rendered: The Benefits of Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs)

content.rendered:
[et_pb_section fb_built=”1″ admin_label=”section” _builder_version=”4.16″ global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_row admin_label=”row” _builder_version=”4.16″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_column type=”4_4″ _builder_version=”4.16″ custom_padding=”|||” global_colors_info=”{}” custom_padding__hover=”|||”][et_pb_text admin_label=”Text” _builder_version=”4.27.4″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”]

What’s the play?

Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs) are rapidly gaining popularity across the country as versatile, impactful solutions to housing and lifestyle challenges. Many homeowners are starting their search by exploring the benefits of accessory dwelling units, since these structures can support family needs, generate extra income, and increase property value. Here, we break down the considerations, options, personal impact, and social impact of building an ADU.

1. Blind Spots

Before diving into construction, thoughtful planning is crucial for a successful ADU project. Many people mistakenly assume they can buy a modular home on Amazon and simply place it on their property. Do not do that! Here’s what you may want to start thinking about:

  • Local Regulations: This part sucks, but zoning laws, setback requirements, and permit processes must be addressed. But here’s some good news: it’s getting better. For instance, the City of Phoenix, Arizona, recently updated its regulations to make building ADUs more accessible. These new rules eased setback requirements, allowing ADUs to be built closer to property lines—a significant shift. Traditionally, zoning laws required substantial distances between the primary residence, property lines, and additional structures. This change benefits urban lots where space is limited, enabling more homeowners to construct ADUs without needing variances and streamlining the approval process.

  • Space and Layout: Assess your lot size and how an ADU will integrate with your existing property. Consider architectural design controls and how the ADU will complement your existing home to maximize enjoyment and property value.

  • Budget: Establish a clear budget, accounting for design, construction, permits, and unforeseen costs. For example, in Phoenix, you might budget $10,000 for site preparation and $15,000 for waste and electrical connections (see Utilities & Infrastructure below). Include costs for purchasing the unit, logistics, and finishing details in your planning.

  • Purpose: Clearly define the purpose of your ADU—family housing, rental income, or a home office? Your purpose will shape the design and functionality.

  • Utilities and Infrastructure: Plan for water, sewer, and electrical connections. Depending on your property, utility upgrades may be necessary.

Regulatory changes in places like Phoenix highlight growing support for ADUs as solutions to housing shortages, offering homeowners more flexibility and fewer barriers to construction. Proper planning not only minimizes obstacles but also supports a smooth process.

2. Options for ADUs

ADUs come in various forms, each with unique benefits tailored to different needs:

  • Detached ADU (DADU): A separate structure from the main house, offering the most privacy. Ideal for rental units or multi-generational living.

  • Attached ADU: Built as an addition to your existing home, providing connectivity while maintaining distinct spaces.

  • Garage Conversion: Transform an underutilized garage into a livable space—an affordable and efficient option.

  • Basement or Attic Conversion: Convert existing square footage into functional housing, saving costs on new construction.

  • Prefab ADU: Pre-manufactured units delivered and assembled on-site for faster build times and potential cost savings.

Selecting the right ADU type depends on your property’s characteristics, budget, and intended use.

3. Your Impact

Building an ADU offers tangible benefits for homeowners:

  • Increased Property Value: ADUs add equity, appealing to buyers seeking flexible living options. While construction costs are high, they are often less than the resale value added to your property, making ADUs an attractive investment.

  • Passive Income: Renting your ADU can generate consistent revenue and often offsets construction costs. Growing up in Brooklyn, NY, I saw upwardly mobile young couples buy duplexes for precisely this reason.

  • Enhanced Family Living: ADUs provide private housing for aging parents, adult children, or extended family, allowing independence while fostering community. Duplex living was also popular for building intentional communities with family or friends who valued affordable housing.

  • Flexible Space: ADUs adapt to your needs as home offices, guest suites, or short-term rentals. During COVID-19, many homeowners appreciated having separate spaces for work, play, and living under one roof.

Beyond financial and functional gains, an ADU enriches your lifestyle by adding flexibility and long-term value.

4. Social Impact

ADUs contribute positively to broader social and environmental goals:

  • Housing Affordability: ADUs create lower-cost rental opportunities, easing housing shortages in high-demand areas.

  • Community Growth: Increasing density through ADUs strengthens neighborhoods without resorting to large-scale developments or apartment complexes. This helps preserve the character of communities while offering housing for essential workers like teachers, police officers, and firefighters.

  • Sustainability: Using existing land for housing reduces urban sprawl and promotes resource efficiency.

  • Aging in Place: ADUs allow seniors to remain close to family, reducing the need for costly assisted living facilities.

  • Economic Boost: ADUs generate income for homeowners and create jobs for local contractors, designers, and suppliers.

By building an ADU, you’re not just enhancing your property—you’re contributing to a more sustainable, connected, and inclusive community.

The Net-Net

Accessory Dwelling Units offer a compelling blend of personal, financial, and social benefits. With thoughtful planning and design, an ADU can transform your property while addressing broader housing challenges. Whether you’re adding space for family, generating income, or improving your property’s value, an ADU is a smart and impactful investment.

Ready to get started? Let us help you navigate the process from concept to construction, ensuring your ADU delivers on its full potential. We can be reached at hello@craydl.com.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]

title.rendered: The Largest Homebuilder You’ve Never Heard Of

content.rendered:
When I heard that Sekisui House—a legendary Japanese homebuilder—was stepping into the U.S. homebuilding scene like a well-dressed guest who arrives fashionably late to a party, I thought, “What’s this going to mean for us?” And man, this might be how modular actually makes it’s way to the U.S. at scale. Let’s break it down.

A Brief History: From Tokyo to Tulsa?

Sekisui House has been around since 1960, and they’re no stranger to innovation. They’ve built millions of homes around the world, but it’s not just about throwing up walls and calling it a day. Oh no. They bring in things like zero-emission homebuilding, energy efficiency, and prefabricated components that assemble quickly and with precision. Their goal? To take the U.S. by storm with their embedded efficiencies. Think Datsun, Nissan and Toyota beating us at own own game in quality car manufacturing.

So, what’s their big move? They’ve bought out M.D.C. Holdings for a cool $4.9 billion, making them one of the top five homebuilders in America. But the real question is: How are they going to fit into our unpredictable housing market? Will their fancy Japanese ways mesh with our good old American homebuilding traditions?

Sustainable Homes: Bringing Sexy Back to Prefab

Now, Sekisui’s not just building any old homes—they’re here to bring sustainable homebuilding to the masses. Think less “installing solar panels as an afterthought” and more “your whole house runs cleaner than a Tesla on a sunny day.” Prefabrication, modular construction, and zero-emission practices are their bread and butter. Sure, it’s cutting-edge, but is the U.S. ready to switch from traditional homebuilding to this new wave of construction? Some states are totally on board (looking at you, California), but others? Let’s just say we’re pretty set in our ways in the U.S.. And, it gets tricky: Sekisui’s advanced construction techniques could face regulatory hiccups in places where local codes haven’t caught up with the times. I mean, it’s like trying to tell someone to switch to streaming when they’ve still got a shelf full of VHS tapes. You can do it, but it might take a while. Still, with enough persistence, scale, presence and most importantly, access to cheap risk capital, Sekisui could redefine the rules of the game.

Impact on the Custom Home Market: Can You Have It Your Way?

The custom home market is a unique animal, kind of like when you get to pick your own toppings at a frozen yogurt bar. It’s all about personalization. People want what they want, and they want it done their way. So what happens when a company like Sekisui, known for modular and prefab methods, enters a space that’s all about individualized design?

Now, you might think prefabrication and custom homes are incompatible; but there’s a way to make it work. Sekisui could introduce customizable prefab options that let buyers pick and choose their layouts, materials, and eco-friendly upgrades. This wouldn’t just reduce build times—it would also give people their dream homes with a green twist, at more affordable prices.

State and Local Regulations: The Good, the Bad, and the Red Tape

This isn’t going to be a cakewalk, though. The U.S. regulatory system is about as easy to navigate as the New York City subway system in rush hour. Some states have regulations that will happily roll out the red carpet for Sekisui’s green building techniques. Others? Not so much. And don’t get me started on the fragmented building codes.

Sekisui will have to be nimble, working within the IRC 2018 (International Residential Code) for single-family homes, and gradually introducing elements from IBC 2021 (International Building Code) as sustainable homebuilding catches on. It’s a balancing act, like juggling while riding a unicycle—only with a lot more paperwork.

Three Possible Outcomes: Cue the Drumroll

Alright, let’s lay it out—what are the most likely outcomes of Sekisui’s U.S. expansion? Here’s my take:

      1.    Incremental Adoption of Sustainability:  This is the “slow and steady wins the race” scenario. Sekisui’s eco-friendly methods will gain traction in states with progressive building codes and sustainability incentives. Builders across the U.S. might slowly incorporate some of Sekisui’s techniques, but it won’t be an overnight sensation. Think of it as the tortoise in the race, but the tortoise has a solar panel strapped to its shell.

      2.    Regional Success, National Struggle: In states like California and New York, Sekisui could become the poster child for sustainable homebuilding, but in the more regulation-reluctant regions, they might struggle to break through. They’ll be a hit in the places that care about eco-friendliness, but elsewhere? It could be a tough sell—like trying to tell someone to eat salad at a barbecue.

      3.    A Prefabricated Revolution: The big win: Sekisui’s prefabrication methods catch on like wildfire, transforming how homes are built across the U.S. The custom home market might evolve, too, with more buyers opting for eco-friendly, modular homes that still allow for personal customization. This is the dream scenario, but it’ll take time, smart marketing, and a whole lot of regulatory work .

The Net Net:

So where does this leave us? Well, Sekisui House has the potential to shake things up in a big way. They’re not just bringing in new homes—they’re bringing in new ideas about how we build, live, and sustain ourselves. Whether it’s through prefabrication, modular construction, or sustainability, one thing’s for sure: they’re going to leave their mark. It might take some time, and there will be hurdles, but the company is in an interest position to make a real impact. It’s certainly a company whose progress we’ll be keeping an eye on.

title.rendered: Embracing Change in Architecture and Construction for Sustainability and Relevance

content.rendered:
In every industry, you’ll find them: people who are comfortable doing things the way they’ve always been done. They wear their traditions like a badge of honor. In the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Supply Chain (AECS) world, these individuals resist change, clinging to outdated practices even as technological advances promise to make their lives easier. One of the clearest examples is BIM vs 2D drafting in architecture, where some professionals still rely on outdated methods even as BIM reshapes efficiency, accuracy, and collaboration across the industry. The fading stars of every profession share a common trait: they steadfastly resist the inevitable evolution that could propel them—and their firms—into the future.

In AECS, technology isn’t just an assist, it changes the game. What used to take days or even weeks—calculating loads, drafting designs by hand, coordinating trades—can now be done in minutes. Building Information Modeling (BIM) allows for seamless collaboration, ensuring fewer errors and better efficiency. Laser scanning provides hyper-accurate measurements of job sites, and project management software ensures deadlines aren’t missed, and resources are optimally used. Yet, some in the field dig in their heels, refusing to acknowledge the advantages.

BIM vs 2D Drafting: Why Architects Must Embrace Change

For many architects, the resistance to 3D modeling and BIM comes from a sense of nostalgia. They remember when 2D drafting was the standard, and they know it inside and out. There’s a certain comfort in sticking to what you know, in continuing to use AutoCAD or other 2D tools to sketch out plans and sections.

But as much as 2D drafting has served the industry well in the past, it’s no longer enough to meet the demands of modern projects. Sticking to these methods introduces more room for error—misalignments, miscalculations, and coordination issues with other disciplines that are increasingly moving toward 3D modeling and BIM. While 2D drawings may get the job done, they don’t provide the same level of insight, precision, or collaboration that 3D tools can offer. This is particularly dangerous given that projects are increasing in complexity, due to both choice and updated code compliance

The Goldilocks Effect: Why Change Is Easy to Avoid

We are currently in what you might call a Goldilocks economy. Business is good, projects are flowing, and the wheels of commerce are turning smoothly. In such an environment, there’s little incentive to change. If everything is running well enough using 2D drafting, why take the risk of adopting something new?

When projects are moving quickly and revenue is strong, inefficiencies can be hidden. Minor errors that stem from 2D drafting—like clashes between different systems or rework due to misinterpretations—can be absorbed by the grease that lubricates the wheels of commerce in a strong economy. Revenue can hide a lot of mistakes. But when the economy slows down, and margins tighten, those inefficiencies come to the forefront. It’s like a game of musical chairs—everything seems fine until the music stops, and suddenly you’re left without a chair.

When projects slow down or clients become more cost-conscious, the AECS professionals who have embraced technology will be the ones who can differentiate their offerings and illustrate how information gets managed by potential clients with more choice, while those who rely solely on 2D drafting may find themselves struggling to keep up.

The Hidden Costs of 2D Drafting

The reluctance to move beyond 2D drafting doesn’t just cost time; it can cost money and credibility. While 2D tools are quick and familiar, they lack the depth that 3D models bring to the table. Coordinating a complex project across multiple disciplines becomes more difficult with 2D drawings. Contractors, engineers, and designers must spend extra time cross-referencing different sets of drawings, increasing the risk of miscommunication and errors.

More importantly, 2D drafting limits your ability to perform detailed analysis during the design process. With BIM, for example, you can run clash detection, energy analysis, and even simulate construction sequences—all things that are impossible with 2D drawings alone. These tools allow architects to deliver a higher quality product, with fewer surprises during construction, which is something that 2D drafting simply can’t match.

Investing in Tomorrow: Keeping the Stars Burning

Firms that will continue to shine in the profession are those who are investing in their future. They recognize that adopting new technology isn’t just about buying software—it’s about rethinking processes, reshaping workflows, and making a cultural shift within the firm. The transition from 2D to 3D drafting, and eventually to BIM, isn’t easy. It requires learning new tools, breaking old habits, and putting in the effort to create new procedures. But for those willing to make the investment, the payoff is significant.

Many professionals are already mature in preparing for the inevitable shift in the industry. They know that as projects become more complex, clients will demand more precision, more transparency, and faster turnarounds. And they’ll be able to deliver, not by working harder, but by working smarter—leveraging technology to do more with less.

From Fading Star to Shining Example

The good news is that it’s never too late to embrace change. Many architects who were once reluctant to adopt BIM have found that, after making the switch, they’re able to design better, faster, and with fewer errors. By moving away from 2D drafting, they’ve positioned themselves as leaders in the field, rather than relics of a bygone era.

The future of architecture is bright for those who are willing to evolve. For those still clinging to 2D drafting, the stars are fading fast. But with a willingness to adapt and invest in new tools, even the most hesitant can find themselves shining once again—leading their firms into the future of architectural design and construction.

The Net-Net

The tools available today have the potential to transform the way architects work, making projects faster, more accurate, and more collaborative. Those who continue to rely solely on 2D drafting risk fading into obscurity, holding onto a past that no longer serves them. In a Goldilocks economy, where everything is going well, it’s easy to avoid change. But when the economy shifts, and clients become more demanding, those who have invested in technology will be the ones who thrive.

It’s time to let go of 2D drafting as the default and embrace the future of architectural design. The stars that shine brightest are those that adapt to their ever-changing environments.

title.rendered: BIM vs Traditional Methods in Construction

content.rendered:
BIM (short for building information modeling) has become a buzzword, often thrown around with little understanding of its full potential. Firing up a new technology and not investing in getting the maximum benefit is not unique to architecture, engineering, construction & supply chain (AECS) but, many architects and builders are quick to say they “do BIM,” but what does that really mean? The truth is, there’s BIM, and then there’s BIM. The distinction lies in how the technology is applied and whether it’s used to merely reproduce traditional CAD processes or to unlock the transformative possibilities that true BIM and Virtual Design Construction (VDC) offer.

The Early Pitfalls of Inefficient BIM Usage

My 6-year old niece plays the piano, and while I delight in her enthusiasm and absorption of music, I don’t experience it, or expect to experience it like I do Mozart. In other words, the instrument does not make music without its musician. Just like in music, tool itself is less important that the people using it. There are so many inefficiencies in custom residential projects begin early in the design phase, and could be resolved with a more acute understanding of what BIM can achieve, with the right practitioners involved. Just like my niece playing chopsticks at a perfectly tuned, baby grand Steinway piano, architects who have transitioned from CAD to BIM tools like Revit, only to use them as they used CAD, essentially creating 2D drawings from a generic 3D model, which misses the point of BIM and fails to leverage its full capabilities.

Some firms further compound this gap in understanding further by outsourcing their BIM work to overseas teams, resulting in Revit models built with generic families that are “good enough” for producing construction drawings but lack the detailed information necessary for more advanced functions, and are only permit ready after a lot of intervention from the onshore architects. While using a Revit model offers clear advantages over a series of 2D CAD sheets, the term ‘using a bazooka to kill a mouse’ comes to mind.

Masters of BIM: Going Beyond the Basics

True BIM mastery involves much more than simply using the software to produce construction drawings. At its best, BIM allows for the accurate modeling of what are known as “families” or assemblies of construction materials. This is analogous to assembling a construction detail in CAD, but with a crucial difference: the accuracy and richness of the data in BIM allow for detailed quantification and analysis.

For instance, using well-built families in a BIM model enables precise material takeoffs—a significant improvement over traditional surface material takeoffs. But it doesn’t stop there. Advanced users might model the engineering aspects of the project as well.

Civil Engineering: Optimizing Earthworks and Beyond

Incorporating civil engineering into the BIM model allows for optimization of earthworks, calculating the amount of dirt import or export required to bring the planned construction to the finished floor grade. This level of detail can significantly impact cost estimation and construction logistics, leading to more informed decision-making early in the project.

MEP & Structural Modeling: Early Detection and Value Engineering

Systems modeling—encompassing Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing (MEP), and Structural components—opens the door to early value engineering. By accurately modeling these systems, potential issues can be detected and addressed long before they become costly problems on the job site. Moreover, this approach enables precise quantification of materials such as lumber, HVAC components, electrical wiring, and plumbing fixtures.

Structural engineering detailing within BIM can even extend to hardware and steel fabrication needs, allowing for prescriptive testing of constructability, clash and conflict analysis, and more. This level of detail ensures that the project is not only feasible but optimized for efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

Interior Design & Landscape Modeling: Enhancing Client Experience

Perhaps the most significant advantage of a fully realized BIM process is its impact on the client experience. By incorporating interior design and landscape modeling into the BIM environment, all potential conflicts between these elements and the architecture or engineering can be resolved early on. Clients can visualize their spaces in a way that was previously impossible, helping to address any sensitivities or preferences before construction begins.

This holistic approach not only enhances the client’s understanding and satisfaction but also helps control costs and timelines by reducing the likelihood of changes during construction.

BIM That Delivers Outcomes

In the end, the difference between “doing BIM” and truly mastering BIM can be the difference between a successful project and one riddled with inefficiencies and unexpected costs. By fully embracing the capabilities of BIM and VDC, architects and builders can optimize every aspect of the design-build process, from earthworks to interior design, ensuring a smoother, more predictable path to completion. There’s BIM, and then there’s BIM. The choice is yours.

title.rendered: How BI and BIM Help Custom Builders Improve Efficiency and Profit

content.rendered:
[et_pb_section fb_built=”1″ admin_label=”section” _builder_version=”4.16″ global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_row admin_label=”row” _builder_version=”4.16″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_column type=”4_4″ _builder_version=”4.16″ custom_padding=”|||” global_colors_info=”{}” custom_padding__hover=”|||”][et_pb_text admin_label=”Text” _builder_version=”4.16″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”]

In the evolving landscape of custom home construction, builders are increasingly gathering new insights to optimize their operations. Small custom home builders can now accelerate their business intelligence (BI) by integrating it with Building Information Modeling (BIM) data. This integration drives measurable improvements in speed and accuracy, resulting in higher customer satisfaction and improved project margins.

The Intersection of BI and BIM in Custom Home Building

Business Intelligence (BI) has traditionally been utilized by large corporations to analyze vast datasets and drive strategic decisions. However, the rise of advanced technologies and the growing accessibility of BI tools have enabled small custom home builders to harness these insights. When integrated with Building Information Modeling (BIM) data, BI becomes a powerful tool that transforms raw data into actionable intelligence.

Driving Efficiency and Accuracy

Integrating BI with BIM streamlines operations by identifying inefficiencies in workflows, such as bottlenecks in the supply chain or delays in the construction process. BI tools provide insights into these issues, offering solutions to improve efficiency. For instance, predictive analytics can forecast potential delays based on historical data, allowing builders to proactively address these challenges.

Additionally, BIM enhances the accuracy of delivery through detailed, three-dimensional representations of building projects. BI provides the analytical capability to interpret this data, ensuring that every aspect of the construction process is meticulously planned and executed, minimizing errors and rework. This precision speeds up project completion and enhances the quality of the finished product.

Enhancing Customer Satisfaction

Customer satisfaction is paramount in custom home construction. Homeowners expect their dream homes to be delivered on time and within budget, without compromising on quality. The integration of BI and BIM allows builders to meet these expectations more effectively by providing real-time updates and transparent communication throughout the project, keeping homeowners informed and engaged.

Moreover, this data-driven approach enables builders to offer more accurate cost estimates and timelines, reducing the likelihood of surprises and fostering trust with clients. Satisfied customers are more likely to recommend builders, leading to increased business and a stronger market reputation.

Improving Project Margins

The ultimate goal for any business is to improve profitability. By leveraging BI and BIM, custom home builders can enhance their project margins through increased efficiency and accuracy, which lead to cost savings by reducing rework and delays. Additionally, precise cost estimates help secure better pricing from suppliers and subcontractors.

The insights gained from BI inform strategic decisions, such as optimizing resource allocation and identifying the most profitable projects. This data-driven approach ensures that builders focus their efforts on areas that yield the highest returns, ultimately boosting their bottom line.

Embracing the Future of Construction

As the construction industry continues to evolve, the integration of advanced technologies like BI and BIM will become increasingly essential. Builders who embrace these tools will be better positioned to thrive in a competitive market, delivering superior results for their clients and achieving greater business success.

The clarity provided by BI and BIM is transforming the construction landscape, turning data into a powerful asset that drives efficiency, accuracy, profitability, and scale. For custom home builders, the possibilities are endless, and the details and moving parts are manageable.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]

title.rendered: Outcomes in Home Design and Construction with BIM in Residential Construction

content.rendered:
The Role of Technology in Residential Construction

Many industries have embraced technology to enhance planning and manage outcomes, yet the AEC sector, particularly in the U.S., has been slow to adopt these advancements. For technology to gain traction in this industry, it must drive efficiencies and deliver immediate, measurable results. The reluctance to experiment is understandable given the industry’s razor-thin profit margins and the fact that construction companies spend less than 2% of their gross margin on technology.

Patric Hellermann’s blog post, “The P&L of the Construction Industry,” highlights several critical points about the adoption of software as a service (SaaS) in the AEC industry:

  • Profit Margins: Construction operates with minimal profit margins.

  • Technology Spending: The industry allocates less than 2% of its gross margin to technology, while services to the industry receive 50 times this amount.

  • Outcome-Driven: This is a project-based business that prioritizes tangible outcomes.

Challenges and Solutions in Information Management

A recent report revealed that 70% of construction employees spend up to 20 hours a week seeking information instead of performing their primary job functions. In custom homes, this inefficiency is evident as time is wasted reconciling conflicting details from consultants and designers, establishing ordering quantities, and communicating with subcontractors and suppliers.

Consider the example of tile installation in a shower. Information on this task comes from multiple sources:

  • Architect: Provides wall dimensions, niches, and plumbing fixture locations.

  • Interior Designer: Selects tile, grout color, terminations, and precise fixture locations.

  • Homeowner: Influences pricing and style.

  • Systems Engineering: Identifies potential existing systems in walls and foundations.

These details must be communicated to various suppliers, installers, and subcontractors, each requiring specific information for their part of the project. A purchasing manager like Sarah might need details on tile quantities, while a tile installer like Marcus needs the installation pattern. This fragmented information can be streamlined using a digital twin and live construction models, ensuring that updates made by any party are instantly available to all stakeholders.

The Future with BIM and Digital Twins

Effective project management today relies on gathering and managing all AEC information in a digital twin, structuring project data in a live construction model. This approach ensures fewer mistakes, timely execution, and reduced hours spent chasing information, benefiting builders, architects, interior designers, homeowners, and developers alike.

Join Our Community

For more insights into the intersection of Building Information Modeling (BIM) and U.S.-based residential design-build, subscribe to our newsletter. Stay updated on how Craydl’s preconstruction services and technological solutions can transform your next project.

title.rendered: Designing and Building Custom Hillside Homes in Phoenix and Beyond

content.rendered:
Interview by Nicholas Tsontakis of Dwell Boldy with Adam Katz of Craydl

Designing and building a custom hillside home can be a challenging yet rewarding endeavor. To shed light on this complex process, we spoke with Adam Katz, the founder of Craydl, a company dedicated to making the custom home-building experience transparent, collaborative, and efficient. Here, Adam shares his insights and expertise.

Tell us a little about Craydl, its mission, and its purpose.

I’ve been buying, designing, building, developing, and selling custom homes for thirty years. Originally, Craydl was going to be a full-service design-build company, but after doing a lot of market research, I learned two things:

  1. Phoenix didn’t need another design-build firm; we have excellent architecture and construction organizations with deep talent.

  2. The technology and processes needed to make designing and building more transparent and streamlined are available and utilized all around the world. Unfortunately, the adoption among homebuilders in the US has been slow, especially among custom homebuilders. Embracing building information modeling (BIM), information management, and effective program management has been even slower.

I quickly became interested in why there was a lack of interest, and what I learned was that putting out fires takes priority over installing systems for process improvements. Everyone’s working in their own systems (CAD, ArchiCAD, Chief Architect, drawings by hand), and making an accurate and inclusive BIM model for a project seems like a great idea but seems to wind up in the bin of things that ‘should be someone else’s problem’. Today, Craydl bridges that gap by taking each participant’s work and adding it to a digital twin the BIM in Revit. Doing this allows us to offer key insights, transparency, and controls into the design/build process.

In short, Craydl exists to make the journey of creating a custom home understandable, transparent, collaborative, auditable, efficient, and fun for ALL stakeholders in the process by creating a unified version of the truth. We do this by building a unique program for and a digital twin of the project.

What is your background in the building industry? How does your background relate to what you are creating with Craydl?

I went to school for architecture and business management. My career has included being a general contractor, design lead, project manager, procurement officer, and more. I don’t know of a part of designing and building a home that I have not been involved with. The process of finding, synthesizing, reconciling, and communicating all of the information from all of the stakeholders was frustrating at best.

Notably, my experience outside of real estate involved a lot of software and IT systems work, specifically the analysis and implementation of software through enterprise programs to drive automation and cleaner data. That data offers business intelligence and provides actionable insights for an enterprise. In the design-build business, it felt like I’d spend my time reacting to problems constantly, and that’s understandable. As I’m often told, that’s just the way this business works. As one executive from a home-building company once told me, ‘if you aren’t getting sued, you aren’t building houses.’ But my background in consulting and technology taught me otherwise and left me asking questions like: how does the accumulation of that knowledge benefit future projects? And, how do we continuously improve? My two worlds collided for me when I began to see the issues not as construction management problems, but as information management problems.

Building information modeling is not new to architecture, especially at the commercial and institutional levels. Why is it important in custom residential?

Custom residential is constantly increasing in complexity with changes in codes and standards, the introduction of new building materials, and new technologies. Furthermore, and often misunderstood by homeowners, it takes work and information from many more resources than just your homeowner, architect, and builder. There are another 17 roles that go into the planning of a home. There are approximately 100 subcontractors that will work on your project. The number of decisions that need to get made is in the tens of thousands. Not only do the decisions need to get captured, but they need to be incorporated into the plan set AND they must be modified when there are conflicts or updated decisions made.

This is a tremendous amount of work for a single entity or party to do using traditional methods, and this will create many risks to a project, including ordering extra materials, not enough materials, untimely value engineering, delays, mistakes, and more. So many of the issues, pain points, and frankly, unforced errors that people associate with projects gone wrong can be solved with strong BIM and program management.

How much more important is it when dealing with complicated builds like hillside homes?

Hillside projects have more complexity and more moving parts. The greater the complexity, the greater the need to model and measure. One immediate example that comes to mind is the optimization of cut/fill. In other words, how much dirt do we need to remove or add to the job site after excavation at $85 per cubic yard? Being able to measure that upfront can often provide enough cost savings to pay for the BIM services alone.

I understand that you are passionate about information modeling. How does this help someone at the beginning stages of designing a custom hillside home?

I’m passionate about information management, which starts before we even get to start using building information modeling. Before BIM can start, we flush out the program-level questions first and help establish a reasonable budget using some other tools we’ve created. We also assemble the program, which means if you have your architect, builder, and interior designer, we are going to also make sure that civil, stormwater and drainage, landscape, structural, MEP, IECC, and the rest of the village required to drive a successful outcome are known, selected, and on board with the program and schedule to mitigate risk and drive more successful outcomes. This often gets overlooked or regarded as not necessary upfront; an incorrect notion to be sure. You would not want to listen to a symphony orchestra play if some of the horn players were going to show up and start playing in the middle of the concert, never having read the sheet music or met the conductor.

Give us an idea of how this cost estimator works? And also how it gets refined throughout the process.

I look at budgeting and estimating as two distinctly different activities. Budgeting happens before plans are created and establishes budget categories that align with the desired scope and objectives of the homeowner. We do have a budgeting calculator that allows us to pick a program (i.e., New Home) and answer approximately 60 questions that homeowners typically understand how to answer. At the end of it, based on the answers to the questions, the calculator provides a line-item budget in conjunction with the answers to the questions to memorialize the scope that drove the budget. If the budget is not desirable, the answers to the questions can be modified to align the scope with the desired budget. Estimating is an activity to be conducted by the contractor once all design decisions are made, finalized, documented, and approved.

Want to learn more about what Craydl has to offer? Check out our other blogs!

title.rendered: BIM in Interior Design Implementation

content.rendered:
Streamlined Communication Creates Stunning Results

How can BIM make communication better

Effective communication is essential for successful project execution in the world of interior design. Imagine a scenario where changing the location of a sconce automatically updates all relevant documents, including elevations, renderings, material schedules, and lighting plans. This seamless integration is achievable with Building Information Modeling (BIM), which provides a structured approach to managing information, reducing conflicts and miscommunication. In this blog, we will explore how interior designers can collaborate with a BIM or virtual design construction (VDC) partner to create top-notch documentation and immersive 3D and VR experiences for clients.

Finding a Trusted BIM Partner and Defining the Workflow

Start by identifying a trusted BIM partner who will work with you to develop a clear communication workflow. This workflow should outline what you, as the interior designer, need to provide to achieve a successful outcome. Key considerations include the architectural work or existing conditions information required and the responsible party for its provision. Furthermore, establish how interior design selections and furnishings details will be communicated and through which systems. When used effectively, BIM can generate schedules based on architectural work, facilitating a list of base selections that need to be made.

Providing Your Assets and an Example of Excellence

To ensure the best results, supply your BIM partner with examples of high-quality specification books to emulate. Additionally, provide your logo, title block, and branding standards to be incorporated into the document production. Be open to modifying your workflow to enhance efficiency.

Deliverables you should expect from your BIM partner

In return, you should receive comprehensive material schedules, 3D visualizations, and construction documentation, including floor plans, reflected ceiling plans, elevations of all walls, and furniture plans. This approach allows you to spend less time drafting and more time refining the design to meet your clients’ needs.

Discover more about how Craydl can support your projects. Learn about the importance of BIM in our blog “What is BIM and Why Should Custom Home Builders and High-End Remodeling Companies Use It?” for a deeper understanding of how Building Information Modeling can revolutionize your projects.

By effectively integrating BIM into your interior design workflow, you can ensure stunning results and a seamless experience for your clients.

excerpt.rendered: This approach allows you to spend less time drafting and more time refining the design to meet your clients’ needs.

title.rendered: The Future of Connected Construction and Smart Building Technology

content.rendered:
Embracing Advanced Technologies for Superior Construction Outcomes

Meet Frank, a seasoned plumber arriving at the job site equipped with more than just traditional tools. His hard hat is embedded with a state-of-the-art scanner and an augmented reality (AR) lens. As he steps into the bathroom, where he had installed vent pipes the previous day, his AR lens alerts him to a potential issue: a flashing red light at the bottom of the wall. Moving closer, Frank sees he missed a nail plate, a critical oversight that could lead to inspection or performance failure.

Imagine a construction site transformed by continuous data collection and real-time improvement. In this future, data becomes seamlessly integrated into every aspect of the process, fundamentally changing how we detect and manage defects. It’s essential to understand that in construction, every created, designed, and produced element is data. The key distinction lies in whether this data is unstructured or structured.

Why Structured Data Is the Foundation of Connected Construction

Unstructured data is inaccessible and unorganized, often existing as stacks of documents or PDF files encompassing architecture, interior design, engineering, and more. Builders typically synthesize this data to solicit accurate bids and estimates. For instance, if Jason in purchasing needs to determine the amount of tile for a primary shower floor, he must sift through documents to find the required information. This process is repeated for various materials like roofing and drywall, consuming significant time and resources. Additionally, discrepancies between architectural and interior design plans can lead to confusion and errors.

The Solution: Building Information Modeling (BIM) and the Power of Data Organization

BIM, when implemented correctly, structures data efficiently. For example, the floor tile specification includes detailed information such as the type of tile, cost, quantity, and an allowance for waste. This structured data is readily available in reports, ensuring consistency and accuracy across all project documents. By leveraging BIM, every detail—from flooring to cabinetry and mechanical systems—is systematically organized, reducing errors and saving time.

Frank’s Enhanced Experience

Advanced technologies like LiDAR scanners and AR lenses enable continuous, precise data collection from the construction site. These tools create detailed 3D models and capture parametric information in real-time, transforming data collection into a continuous stream. This real-time information can be compared to the BIM, enabling automated defect detection and inspection tracking. Discrepancies between as-built conditions and planned designs are immediately flagged, allowing for early issue resolution and minimizing costly rework and delays.

Streamlining Code Compliance Through Automation

Structured data also streamlines code compliance. Traditionally, ensuring compliance involves manual checks using spreadsheets and punch lists. In the future, this process will be automated, with continuous data streams analyzed for compliance with relevant codes and standards. This automation increases accuracy, saves time, and ensures projects meet regulatory requirements effortlessly.

Long-Term Benefits of Continuous Data Collection

Continuous data collection creates a comprehensive record of the construction process, providing valuable information for future maintenance and renovations. This holistic approach to information management ensures buildings remain efficient and safe throughout their lifecycle.

The Path Forward for Connected Construction and Smart Building

The future of intelligent design-build and connected construction lies in building a strong BIM foundation and leveraging data to drive superior outcomes. By embracing cutting-edge technologies and innovative practices, data becomes the cornerstone of decision-making, improving efficiency, and enhancing quality.

This future isn’t far off—it’s within reach. Embrace the potential of advanced technologies and transform the way you build.

The future of construction is connected. CRAYDL helps builders and developers harness BIM, AR, and real-time data to build smarter and more efficiently.

excerpt.rendered: Imagine a construction site transformed by continuous data collection and real-time improvement.

title.rendered: “All Swedish No Finish”. Why Custom Home Projects Stall Before the Finish Line

content.rendered:
Custom Homes Turn to Dream Graveyard

Athletics have always been fiercely competitive, often accompanied by a fair share of heckling. An amusing jab from the past, “All Swedish, No Finish,” emerged when Europeans were once deemed too soft for North American hockey. This playful taunt persists among players, humorously highlighting the physical decline that comes with aging. Today, the phrase also captures why custom home projects stall or go into a dream graveyard, especially in complex builds where strong ideas fail to reach the finish line.

Why Custom Home Projects Stall Before Construction Begins

While obvious reasons may quickly come to mind, the subtleties behind them are crucial to understand. Each challenge demands specific people, processes, and tools for resolution. Moreover, our own humanity and cognitive biases add an often-unnoticed layer of distortions.

Obstacle 1: Financial Challenges

Budget Overruns

Many architected homes remain unbuilt due to budget overruns. Initial cost estimates often fail to account for unexpected expenses, leading to significant financial strain. Unforeseen costs can arise from material price increases, labor shortages, or changes in project scope. This financial unpredictability can halt progress, as homeowners and builders struggle to secure additional funding. The COVID-19 pandemic exemplified this, causing supply chain disruptions and inflationary pressures on materials and labor. While black swan events are unpredictable, changes in scope or missed details are often avoidable.

Financing Challenges

Securing financing for custom homes can be complex. Lenders may hesitate to finance projects with unconventional designs or those in less developed areas. The stringent requirements and high-interest rates associated with construction loans can deter potential homeowners. Inflationary pressures often lead to higher interest rates, making it essential to plan for this risk when applicable.

Obstacle 2: Planning and Permitting Hurdles

Regulatory Compliance

Navigating the intricate web of zoning laws, building codes, and permits can be daunting. Regulatory bodies require detailed plans adhering to local codes, and obtaining approvals can be time-consuming and costly. Deviations or modifications may necessitate further reviews and delays. A thorough understanding of municipal and architectural control body expectations is crucial for navigating this regulatory labyrinth.

Environmental and Site Issues

Site-specific challenges such as unstable soil, flood zones, or protected wildlife habitats can significantly impede construction plans. Environmental regulations often demand extensive site surveys and impact assessments, adding complexity and expense. These issues may lead to project redesigns or, in extreme cases, abandonment of the site. A proficient team with clear expectations management is indispensable to ensure smooth project progression.

Obstacle 3: Communication and Coordination Breakdowns

Stakeholder Misalignment

Effective communication among architects, builders, and homeowners is crucial. Misunderstandings can result in undocumented or unapproved design changes, leading to costly rework. Ensuring all parties are aligned on the project’s vision, scope, and execution plan is essential to avoid delays and conflicts. Today, builders use tools like BuilderTrend for daily logs, selection controls, and budget and schedule transparency.

Changes in Design and Scope

Homeowners often change their minds about design elements during the project. While flexibility is necessary, frequent changes can disrupt the construction timeline and inflate costs. Architects and builders must manage these changes carefully, balancing homeowner desires with practical construction considerations. Tools like BuilderTrend effectively manage change orders, while platforms like Craydl establish reasonable budgets from the onset.

Obstacle 4: Construction and Logistical Challenges

Builder Availability and Reliability

Finding a reliable builder who is available when needed can be challenging. Builders often juggle multiple projects, and delays in one can cascade into others. The quality and reliability of builders can vary, impacting workmanship and project management. Understanding the builder’s team, systems, and processes is critical.

Material and Labor Shortages

The construction industry’s supply and demand for skilled labor in trades like electricians and plumbers is increasingly constrained. Builders rely on established relationships with trades and bench depth to scale up as work ramps and maintain quality control. However, supply chain disruptions, especially during economic uncertainty or global events, exacerbate these issues.

Obstacle 5: Cognitive Biases and Decision-Making Pitfalls

Overconfidence Bias

Homeowners and architects can sometimes be overly optimistic about their ability to manage complex projects, leading to underestimating risks and costs.

Anchoring Bias

Anchoring bias occurs when stakeholders fixate on initial estimates or plans, making it difficult to adapt to changing circumstances.

Status Quo Bias

There is often a preference for maintaining the current state rather than taking on construction challenges. This resistance to change counteracts continuous improvement.

How Builders Can Reduce Risk and Keep Projects Moving

To mitigate cognitive biases, stakeholders can adopt several strategies:

Comprehensive Planning

A detailed and realistic planning process that includes robust cost estimates, contingency plans, and a clear timeline helps manage expectations and reduce budget overruns.

Transparent Communication

Regular and transparent communication among all parties ensures alignment and prompt resolution of issues. Building Information Modeling (BIM) provides a single source of truth for all project data.

Flexible Adaptation

Being open to adjustments and flexible in response to changing conditions helps keep projects on track. A team ready to absorb new information and improved processes is crucial.

Bringing Custom Home Projects Across the Finish Line

The journey from architectural vision to a completed home can and should be understandable, transparent, and successful. Leveraging tools like BIM and Virtual Design & Construction (VDC) creates a single source of truth, driving accurate information management, reducing errors, and enhancing coordination. By adopting a programmatic approach to preconstruction services, architects, builders, and homeowners can achieve their dream projects with greater confidence and efficiency.

By addressing these obstacles with a strategic approach, custom home projects can transition from dreams to reality, ensuring both beauty and functionality are achieved… and finished.

excerpt.rendered: The phrase “All Swedish, No Finish” aptly describes many custom home projects—a veritable graveyard of unfulfilled dreams.

title.rendered: Drafting Chaos and the Cost to Architecture Firms

content.rendered:
Why Drafting Chaos Exists in Architecture Firms

For architects and interior designers, the intersection of art and science demands precision and creativity, often generating a significant amount of drafting work. Drafting chaos in architecture firms often stems from staffing gaps, repetitive production tasks, and constant change. Finding diligent, experienced, and committed drafting resources can be challenging and resource-intensive. Many architecture and interior design firms struggle to develop top-tier talent due to the uninspiring nature of entry-level tasks. This results in high turnover, with departing individuals often labeled as underperformers or uncommitted, while remaining team members become overworked. Consequently, managers face the draining, expensive, and unfulfilling task of continuously training new, underperforming individuals.

The Real Drafting Challenges Facing Architecture Firms

One architecture client summarizes the environment perfectly: modern architectural firms demand precision and creativity but are constantly bogged down by the inefficiencies of changing team members, material choice complexity, code compliance updates, and client and extended team requests. This scenario stifles innovation and growth, creating an untenable situation.

The Solution: Outsourcing Drafting Work to a U.S.-Based BIM Partner

Outsourcing drafting work to a U.S.-based BIM (Building Information Modeling) partner offers a transformative solution. By taking a programmatic approach, firms can unlock several key advantages:

How BIM Automation Expands Drafting Capacity

Leveraging BIM allows firms to automate much of the sheet production process, including standardizing notations, dimensions, call-outs, and details. Automation in sheet production increases capacity, ensures consistency and accuracy, and frees top talent to focus on critical, creative tasks.

Imagine your best architects channeling their energy into groundbreaking designs rather than mundane drafting tasks. This shift not only boosts morale but also enhances productivity and innovation within the firm.

Managing Engineering Changes Without Rework

A significant pain point in the design process is managing changes during engineering. For instance, when beam sizes are estimated based on floor plans and elevations, they can be remodeled upon the completion of structural engineering. This early fail-safe allows architects to review potentially over-engineered work, significantly impacting costs and affordability.

Predictability is crucial. By comparing initial designs with final structural engineering estimates, firms can catch discrepancies early, preventing costly rework and ensuring projects stay on budget and on schedule. This enhances both morale and the firm’s brand and reputation.

Using BIM to Deliver Faster, More Compelling Visuals

BIM enables the automation of 3D rendering work, creating immersive and interactive experiences. This differentiation not only sets your firm apart but also engages clients early in the process. Realistic renderings help identify client sensitivities and preferences, enhancing communication and satisfaction.

Engaging clients early builds trust and fosters a collaborative atmosphere where feedback is seamlessly incorporated. Many architects already invest in rendering, but with BIM, the ability to provide compelling visuals is dramatically accelerated.

What Architecture Firms Gain by Outsourcing Drafting

Outsourcing drafting work to a BIM partner can revolutionize how architecture and design firms operate. It brings automation, predictability, and enhanced client engagement to the forefront, allowing firms to focus on what they do best: creating stunning, innovative designs.

The future of architecture is here, centered on leveraging technology to streamline processes and elevate creativity. Embrace this transformative approach to extract chaos from your operation and unlock your firm’s full potential. Let’s navigate this journey together.

Learn more about how BIM can transform your practice by contacting Craydl.

title.rendered: Why Home Feasibility Matters Before You Renovate

content.rendered:
Why Should Carl Care About Home Feasibility?

My neighbor Carl and his family are relocating to a new property at the border of Phoenix and Scottsdale. Nestled in a charming enclave with older homes on expansive lots, Carl’s new acquisition holds great potential. But like many homeowners, Carl underestimated the importance of home renovation feasibility when planning an extensive remodel and expansion. Over a casual conversation, Carl revealed his plans to renovate the house extensively, adding more space to enhance functionality. Additionally, he intends to move his equipment from a commercial storage facility to a new structure on the property.

Carl’s real estate agent, who has some proficiency in CAD, supports his vision. Carl has already ordered a civil survey and consulted with a builder, confident that his $700,000 budget will suffice. Here’s a snippet of our conversation:

Carl: What do you think?

Me: About what?

Carl: The plan?

Me: What plan?

Carl: The plan I just told you about.

Me: Well, that’s not a plan; it’s barely an educated guess.

Carl: What would you do?

Me: I would take feasibility seriously.

Why Home Renovation Feasibility Gets Overlooked

I recommended that Carl assemble a team to conduct a feasibility analysis to answer critical questions and make informed decisions. Building this team with the expectation of obtaining data-backed answers is crucial. This team should also remain involved through the project’s completion, ensuring it is fully engineered, designed, and permitted.

Carl: Sounds expensive.

Me: Compared to your agent who knows CAD, probably. But when weighed against the risk of being significantly wrong—potentially $700,000 wrong—it’s a wise investment.

Carl: What would that look like?

Me: Take a look at Craydl’s blogs and read ‘Good Program, Good Outcome.’ I detail the necessary resources.

What a Feasibility Study Should Answer

Below, we delve deeper into project feasibility and its role in projects like Carl’s, helping to answer essential questions such as:

  • How much time and effort will the project require?

  • What will be the real cost of the project?

  • How long will it take to obtain approvals and permits?

  • What is the realistic timeline for completing the project?

  • What additional resources will be needed for approvals and permits?

  • Who will keep Carl informed, and how?

 

Program Management Keeps Feasibility Grounded in Reality

Effective program management, often referred to by titles like project architect, BIM manager, or owner’s representative, is vital for custom projects. A program manager is the conductor of your project’s symphony, maintaining the tempo during preconstruction and driving accountability for every detail. This role is crucial for managing project data and ensuring that specific questions are answered.

For Carl’s project, a good program manager would start by addressing questions such as:

  • Can the home extension be done, and where on the lot can it be built?

  • Are there any association requirements?

  • Can Carl’s property be used for commercial storage?

  • Are there stormwater and drainage needs?

  • What will the home look like?

  • Is $700,000 a reasonable budget?

How Architects Shape Feasible Renovation Plans

Architects provide cohesive planning, regulatory compliance, and integration of the property into its environment. During feasibility, architects develop concept designs, floor plans, and massing designs. They answer questions like:

  • What will the space’s flow feel like?

  • What will the floor plan look like?

  • What will the home look like when complete?

  • Where are all the rooms and functional spaces?

  • How will the modifications support the desired aesthetic?

Civil Engineering Constraints That Impact Renovations

Civil engineers optimize site layout, utilities, and access, ensuring functionality and sustainability. They address questions such as:

  • How much can we add to the home in each direction (setbacks)?

  • Are there areas that cannot be built on (right of ways, easements, native plant surveys)?

  • Where do utilities enter the lot, and is there adequate service?

  • What is the natural grade and topography?

  • Are there earth retention needs?

  • Are stormwater and drainage runoff issues present?

Why Builders Matter Early in Feasibility Planning

A solid contractor is instrumental in developing a strong program from the onset. They can answer questions such as:

  • How do design decisions impact overall cost?

  • How do design decisions affect constructability?

  • How do design decisions influence lead times and the overall timeline?

  • What tools are used to manage the construction process?

Early Structural Input Prevents Costly Surprises

Though less involved during feasibility, structural engineers provide valuable early input on primary structural elements. They can answer questions such as:

  • What beams and girders are needed to support the design?

  • What footing sizes are required for weight-bearing loads?

Feasibility Is Cheaper Than Being Wrong

Behavioral economists have found that cognitive biases, such as optimism bias, can lead people to underestimate risks and challenges. This bias can significantly impact project planning and execution. Carl should avoid oversimplifying during planning, as it can result in complexity and pain later. Instead, conducting a thorough feasibility analysis with a skilled team can lead to informed decisions and successful project outcomes.

For more insights, subscribe to our blog, where we continue to explore the importance of comprehensive planning and feasibility in home construction projects.

title.rendered: A Programmatic Approach for Your Major Remodel or New Home Build

content.rendered:
When taking on a major home remodel or new construction, a programmatic approach to home construction can keep the complexity from becoming overwhelming, but it doesn’t have to be. What we often associate as ‘construction problems’ can sometimes be attributed to poor planning and information management problems. Let’s start with the poor planning piece.

A structured programmatic approach ensures that each step of your project is thoughtfully planned and executed because you built a team with a plan in the first place and manage your project effectively from the ground up.

Steps to a Programmatic Approach

1. Feasibility Assessment: Begin by assessing whether your project goals are realistic. This involves evaluating the potential footprint against the recorded lot and zoning limitations, desired features, style, and of course, a budget that is realistic and detailed with all needed cost categories necessary to complete your project. This foundational step sets the tone for all decisions that follow.

2. Civil Work and Architectural Concepting: Next, focus on the positioning of your lot and develop floor plans, massing, and elevations. It’s essential to ensure that the design of your home harmonizes with the surrounding environment, setting the stage for detailed planning.

3. Navigating Regulatory Requirements: Before proceeding further, understand that specific areas, like those with homeowners associations, hillsides, or architectural control committees, may require additional approvals. Securing these approvals early can prevent costly delays and ensure that your project adheres to all local regulations.

4. Detailing Phase: With architectural approvals in hand, delve into the specifics of architectural details, interior design, engineering, and landscaping. This phase is about refining your vision into detailed, actionable plans that address both form, fit, and functionality.

5. Construction Drawings and Approvals: Finally, transform your detailed plans into construction-ready drawings. These are crucial for precise building and obtaining the necessary permits from local authorities. This step ensures your project is ready for execution and meets all regulatory standards, while serving as a playbook for your builder. Included in this should be interior design and landscape/hardscape specifications that tie to the architectural plans.

Why a Programmatic Approach Reduces Risk

Adopting a programmatic approach to your home-building or remodeling project helps in structuring the process into manageable phases each with specific objectives and outcomes. This methodical planning reduces the risk of unforeseen issues and ensures that your project progresses smoothly, adhering to both your vision and legal requirements.

Bringing it Home

Through careful planning and a step-by-step approach you can navigate the complexities of home construction or remodeling with confidence. By addressing feasibility, design, regulatory requirements, detailed planning, and final approvals in a sequential manner, you ensure that each aspect of your home is thoughtfully considered and well-executed. Remember, the success of your project hinges not just on the design and construction roles but also on proactive management of how they come together and align around the details that will make your project your home.
