title.rendered: Programmatic Home Construction | How a Good Program Ensures the Best Outcome

content.rendered:
Why a Programmatic Approach to Home Construction Matters

When it comes to home construction, many people assume that hiring a builder is sufficient to ensure a smooth and successful project. This misconception can lead to significant oversights and issues down the line. In reality, numerous disciplines play critical roles in the construction process, and overlooking these can result in poor outcomes. To avoid these pitfalls, a programmatic approach to the construction process is essential, ensuring that all aspects are managed and integrated effectively. In essence, each stakeholder listed below manages their projects, which are part of a larger program, driving efficient information sharing, updates, and collaboration.

The Role of the Architect in Custom Home Design

Your architect is your primary designer, your artist, and your project planning sherpa. They are responsible for translating your vision into a feasible plan. During preconstruction, they develop the concept, including lot positioning, floor plans, access, exterior elevations, massing, and door and window openings, ensuring compliance with local building codes. An architect’s involvement can significantly increase your property’s value, potentially adding up to 10-15% due to improved design and functionality. Moreover, most buyers today seek personal touches and thoughtful designs. However, rookie mistakes, like purchasing plans online or using Instagram photos to communicate with a builder, can cost time and money.

How Builders Manage Costs and Preconstruction Planning

The builder, or general contractor, manages the construction process from start to finish. During preconstruction, they provide cost estimates, develop a construction schedule, and coordinate with subcontractors. Effective preconstruction planning by a builder can reduce overall project costs by up to 20% by identifying potential issues early and ensuring efficient use of resources.

Interior Design as a Critical Part of Home Construction

Interior designers do more than select paint colors and tiles; they ensure that spaces are functional and appropriately scaled. They personalize a vision and develop specific details that pick up where the architect left off. This includes everything from tile terminations and material transitions to lighting placement and the height of fixtures. They work with you to understand your needs and preferences, translating these into a cohesive design that enhances your daily life. Proper interior design can increase the resale value of your home by 5-10% and significantly enhance your enjoyment of the space.

Civil Engineering is Essential for Lot Preparation

Civil engineers focus on the design and construction of the physical environment, ensuring that the building site is properly prepared and that the structure will stand the test of time. Good civil engineering can save significant money on foundation repairs and site preparation, potentially reducing these costs by up to 15%. It also informs much of what architects and other engineers do, such as determining finished floor grades and identifying buildable areas of the lot. Without this information, assumptions may lead to significant expenses and delays, as most municipalities now require civil surveys at a minimum.

Stormwater and Drainage Engineering in Modern Homes

Proper stormwater and drainage management are crucial to prevent flooding and water damage. Engineers in this field design systems to manage rainfall and runoff, protecting your home’s foundation and landscaping from erosion and waterlogging. Effective drainage solutions can save thousands in potential water damage repairs and maintain property value. Many municipalities now require this engineering, and when done correctly, it can dramatically impact lot usage. Neglecting this critical aspect can result in substantial rework of architectural and structural engineering plans.

MPE Engineering and the Benefits of Efficient Systems

Mechanical, Plumbing, and Electrical (MPE) Engineering engineers design and ensure the functionality of mechanical systems (like HVAC), plumbing systems (ensuring safe and efficient water supply and waste disposal), and electrical systems (providing reliable power and lighting). These systems are integral to the comfort and safety of your home. Efficient MPE systems can reduce energy and water bills by up to 30%, providing substantial long-term savings and ensuring you have power capacity where and when you need it.

Structural Engineering and Building a Durable Home

Structural engineers ensure that your home is structurally sound and stable. They design the framework that supports the building, considering factors such as load-bearing capacities and resistance to environmental stresses like earthquakes and strong winds. Homeowners sometimes underestimate the value that a structural engineer can bring by simplifying designs and ensuring that structural members are sound but not oversized, offering substantial savings in framing material costs.

Energy Code Compliance for Efficiency and Value

Energy code and compliance experts ensure that your home meets local energy efficiency standards. They help you choose materials and systems that will reduce energy consumption, lower utility bills, and contribute to a sustainable environment. Homes built to higher energy standards can save homeowners up to 20% annually on energy bills and can increase property value by up to 10%.

How a Program Manager Aligns All Disciplines for Success

A programmatic approach to home construction involves a comprehensive plan that integrates all these disciplines. This means having a project manager or lead professional who understands how to coordinate the various inputs from each expert. This role is crucial for ensuring that nothing falls through the cracks and that all aspects of the construction process are aligned.

The program manager collaborates closely with the architect, builder, and homeowner, ensuring that everyone is on the same page and that the project progresses smoothly. They help to anticipate potential issues, facilitate communication among all parties, and make informed decisions that keep the project on track.

Good Program, Good Outcome in Home Construction

Hiring an architect and a builder is a good start, but it may not be enough to guarantee a successful home construction project. Embracing a programmatic view that includes interior design, civil engineering, stormwater and drainage engineering, MPE engineering, structural engineering, and energy code compliance is essential. By doing so, you ensure that all aspects of the construction process are managed effectively, resulting in a home that is not only beautiful but also functional, durable, and efficient. Having a good program ensures you have a good outcome.

title.rendered: Intelligent Home Construction and the Role of BIM

content.rendered:
In the world of custom home building, understanding the tools and tactics that define success is critical. Here are two stories about two couples, with similar budgets and objectives, but who had remarkably different outcomes—let’s dissect why.

Enter BIM: The Power Player in Modern Construction

First, let’s set the stage with Building Information Management (BIM). This isn’t just technology; it’s a transformative strategy that integrates 3D visualization, material scheduling, and system clash detection. Think of BIM as the Google Maps of construction: it doesn’t just show you the building, it plans the routes of the systems, identifies the traffic jams, and allows for problem solving at a rate that no mere mortal could match. Separately, when these types of problems become visible to homeowners or developers, it’s not exactly welcome news.

The Strategists: Using BIM to Plan, Decide, and Execute

Our first couple, let’s call them our Strategists, they get it.  They looked to utilize BIM from the onset of the process, turning what could be a chaotic build process into a symphony of efficiency. The Architect was able to tell the story of the property with a beautiful structure that not only fits well with the landscape but stands out as an example of what meticulously planned lines, and massing can do for a home and its environment.

Potentially oversized structural members were caught early with revision analysis.  Cut/fill volume was optimized.  And, they were able to make their final interior design choices with confidence by seeing options in their virtually simulated space using augmented reality headsets.  With BIM, the entire team was able to visualize not just spaces but outcomes, aligning every inch of their project with their vision. Material waste? Reduced. Unexpected structural or system clashes? Anticipated and avoided. They’re not just building a home; they’re orchestrating an optimized construction process.

On a less geeky note, they were given the visibility that empowered them to be intentional in getting get more of what they wanted, with finishes and details properly designed and documented for procurement and installation.  Also included in the design consideration was sunlight analysis and at different times of the year, layered lighting options and fixtures, home automation and use of space.  Today, our Strategist couple gets a lot of utility, enjoyment, and serenity within their modest and tastefully appointed new home.  When they first started the project, they had hoped to build the home for between $1.25-1.5 million.  During planning, our Strategists had made deliberate decisions to push the budget categories and make room for some of the features they learned about during the design process.  At $1.5 million they came in right on budget.

Cutting Corners Comes at a Cost

Now, contrast this with the second couple, the Economizers, who believed in old-school tactics that really amount to just cutting corners in hopes it will save money. The thought process was, why hire a designer when you can just pick some photos from Instagram and show the builder what I want?  Armed with the level of ignorance that only a TikTok video can provide, they deemed the architect too expensive and opted for a draftsperson to make changes to a plan set they purchased online. The Economizers were subsequently caught off guard by the requirements from both the city and the local associations architectural control committee. Engineering was considered a value-less obstacle, a piece of red tape put up by the city that just needed to be ‘dealt with’.  And they learned the hard way that interior design is more than just picking out photos of what you like on Pinterest.  It turned out that being able to express the details in a way that the builder and his subcontractors can review, understand and rally around is like, really important stuff, but by the time this realization occurred, it was too late.

By not conducting the civil work early in the process, the project planning and review process took over a year.  Once the permits were in hand, site work began, and the contractor showed up on his second day of the job to find a stop work order from the association for not having approval.  After another two months of delays, site work begins, and the contractor has a change order for $7500 to haul in enough dirt to bring the house to the engineered finished floor level.  When the house was substantially framed, the couple began to make their selections and learned that the exterior door and window package in their budget will take 20 weeks to arrive and this will hold up progress for another couple of months.

With framing up the homeowner can now visualize the home for the first time and that the garage doors really need to be a bit taller, and there is a post in the middle of the view off the back patio that they fell in love with when they bought the land.  They are disappointed to learn that to make these changes now will create a lot of time and expense, and so the Economizers choose to accept that their camper won’t fit in the garage and the view thought would be accentuated now has a vertical post through it.

Several months later, with rough in work complete and finally through inspection, the drywall is now up, and cabinets are going in.  The tile that was ordered a year ago has 100 sf in the order.  The space it needs to cover is actually 140 square feet and the tile is no longer returnable or in production.  A new tile must be found; more expense, more delays.  The cabinets are finally in, and the interior designer (homeowner) realizes they need outlets and lighting in places they hadn’t considered; more expense, more delays.  This couple, convinced they would get their dream house done for $700,000 wound up spending over $1 million, plus soft costs which are now growing as they are in litigation.  The couple feels that there have been several missed deadlines, missed expectations and added costs.  Unfortunately, what was missed was the creation of a realistic budget, a complete preconstruction team and the information to plan for a more successful and less stressful outcome.

The Economic and Aesthetic Impact of BIM

The economic disparity here can’t be overstated. Both couples, with substantially the same goal had very different approaches.  The Strategists played their hand with precision and foresight, thanks to BIM. They invested in expertise and technology that pays dividends in terms of time, material costs, and stress. Their home? A masterpiece of modern design and efficiency.

The Economizers, though? They spent almost the same amount of money per square foot as the Strategists did but ended up with a home that’s a monument to missed opportunities. It’s not just about aesthetic mishaps; it’s about the long-term value of the property. They’ll likely face higher maintenance costs, potential retrofitting down the line, and a lower resale value.

Intelligent Planning Beats Expensive Surprises

In the age of information, ignorance isn’t bliss; it’s painfully expensive and emotional. BIM isn’t just changing the game; it’s the new rulebook for efficient, effective home building. For those building a home, embracing BIM and investing in the right expertise isn’t an expense; it’s an investment in getting it right the first time.

Look, I wouldn’t build a friggen doghouse without an exacting plan, but that’s just me.  And it’s easy to overlook or rush, after all, planning every detail of the home is hard and it takes a lot of work. But without it, your builder will go slow, while you are forced to make panicked decisions instead of good decisions.  Suddenly, the cheap comes out expensive and looks like shit.

Technology like BIM doesn’t just save you money—it makes sure every dollar you spend works harder, smarter, and more efficiently. The tale of these two homes isn’t just a story about construction: it’s a lesson in strategic investment and technological leverage.  It’s a lesson about information management in construction.

Make sure to check out our other blogs and socials!

excerpt.rendered: Two Couples, Two Outcomes: The Secret to Successful Custom Home Building

title.rendered: What Craydl Brings to the Table for Architects

content.rendered:
Based in Scottsdale, Arizona, with a nationwide reach, Craydl offers a range of technology-based solutions and services tailored for boutique architecture firms.  In an industry where the top firms allocate up to 30% of their total headcount to technology, how can smaller firms stay competitive while focusing on their core – architecture & design?  The answer lies in smarter planning, adopting new technologies such as BIM, process automation, and information management, along with new processes including lean practices, continuous improvement, and agility.

Mastering the Art of Program Management and BIM

Why is BIM so important?  Simply stated: when done correctly, it can solve a lot of problems before they occur. You might immediately think ‘clash detection’ and, like a lot of architects conclude, “well that’s neat, but none of my consultants work in BIM.”  That’s where we come in.  We model everyone’s work, and can produce .dwg’s for team members to work in their native tools, and then pull it into the BIM.  Structural engineering, same thing.  MEP, same thing.  Civil… you get the idea.  Even the interior designers work will be pulled into Revit and scheduled.  Not only does this allow for proper clash analysis, but it ensures that downstream design considers upstream design, and that when needed, can communicate required detail-related changes.  As we like to remind our clients, problems that cost a dollar during design to correct would have cost fifty dollars in the field.

The advantage for the builder is that, when properly modeled, material schedules can be extracted from the BIM for accurate take offs and big-time savings.  That is a big differentiator.

Turning Scans into Plans with Cutting-Edge Technology

Scan to BIM services make producing an accurate LOD500 model, complete with photogrammetry a reality, drastically reducing the need for site visits, revisions, and unexpected costs when assessing existing conditions. By harnessing tools like Register360, Revit and Autodesk Construction Cloud, we maintain a streamlined, accessible flow of project data, enabling better decisions and a bulletproof plan.

Visualizing Success with 3D Rendering and Technical Drafting

Providing 3D visualization services becomes much easier when the information is all in the model. Today, not only can you provide clients with wonderful, lifelike still 3D renderings, but you can also now provide videos, virtual reality walkthroughs, schematics and more, so that part of your services include crystal-clear visuals that communicate your vision effectively.

A Project-First Philosophy

Craydl’s project-first approach is about more than just leading-edge tech and best practices—it’s about tailoring our arsenal to meet the unique needs and goals of your firm and its project portfolio. We champion your vision, supporting your team every step of the way to deliver exceptional, tangible results in the residential Design-Build sector.

Elevate Your Architecture with Craydl

At Craydl, we’re more than just a service provider—we’re a partner in the development of your legacy. By joining forces with us, you not only enhance your project outcomes but also set a new standard for preconstruction excellence.

Ready to transform your next project? Let’s make it remarkable—connect with us today and elevate your architectural endeavors to new heights.

title.rendered: Architecture vs Interior Design and Why Collaboration Matters

content.rendered:
[et_pb_section fb_built=”1″ _builder_version=”4.16″ global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_row _builder_version=”4.16″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” global_colors_info=”{}”][et_pb_column type=”4_4″ _builder_version=”4.16″ custom_padding=”|||” global_colors_info=”{}” custom_padding__hover=”|||”][et_pb_text _builder_version=”4.27.4″ background_size=”initial” background_position=”top_left” background_repeat=”repeat” hover_enabled=”0″ global_colors_info=”{}” sticky_enabled=”0″]

On the same day, I had two conversations, one with an architect (let’s call him Zach) and another one with an interior designer (we’ll call her Deanne) about the very same project. What unfolded was a classic example of architecture vs interior design in home design miscommunication with consequences that rippled through their work, affecting even the homeowner.

Miscommunication Between Architecture and Interior Design

Deanne, with a keen eye for ambiance and functionality, decided to reposition several lighting fixtures in her designs to better capture the aesthetic she envisioned for the living spaces. Enthusiastic about her refined vision, she made these adjustments on her digital layouts, but crucially, she did not communicate these changes to Zach. As such, Zach, operating under the original specifications, did not update the electrical plans accordingly.

This oversight became apparent when the construction phase was underway, and it was discovered that the wiring points were all prepared for the original light fixture locations. The mismatch necessitated a series of change orders which, not only delayed the project but also significantly increased the costs. This led to palpable frustration from the homeowner, who was displeased with both the unexpected financial burden and the project’s delay.

The Cost of Poor Collaboration in Home Design

Zach was taken aback by the homeowner’s dissatisfaction. From his perspective, he had followed the specifications he was given. He felt it was unfair to be held accountable for issues stemming from changes he was never informed about. After all, he pondered, how could he incorporate changes he wasn’t aware of?

Meanwhile, Deanne’s frustration was twofold. She was irritated that the initial lighting plan had been drafted without her input, which she believed should have been sought, given her role as the interior designer. Moreover, her planning was further hampered by Zach’s delayed delivery of critical architectural documents. She had repeatedly requested the reflective ceiling plans and elevations, which arrived much later than promised, forcing her to rush through her design process to stay on schedule.

Architecture vs Interior Design and Where the Roles Overlap

As many builders will tell you, sometimes these situations are expensive, and it is up to the builder to break this news to affected homeowners and receive the brunt of the response. This is not a building problem; this is an information management problem.

How Collaboration Prevents Change Orders and Delays

What if we lived in a world where the architects work on a floor plan and massing development was a live model? What if, in this utopian world, once floor plans and elevations were approved by the customer and value engineering concluded, the interior designer received copies of all interior elevations, the Reflective Ceiling Plan (RCP) and the floor plans, and a list of all required selections to begin their work?

What if in this new world, as the interior designer made selections, they went into the same model as the architectural, civil and structural work, and informs Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing (MEP) work? Not only would the interior designer be able to see their work, simulate spaces, and provide options to their clients, but also generate from this 3D body of work, interior design elevations, floor plans and RCP models.

 

Creating a Shared Vision for Homeowners, Architects, and Designers

What if we live in that world? A world where there was a singular version of the truth that all professionals put their work into and generate their documents from, thereby setting up clients and their builders for success?

To live in this world, we will need to stop thinking about this as a construction problem that requires more brute force (more micromanagement, more emails, texts, phone calls) to solve, and start looking at how BIM principles and cross-functional processes can make everyone’s performance better, lives better and drive superior outcomes.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]

title.rendered: Super BIM: Navigating the Shadows of Construction Overcharges with Cutting-Edge Tech

content.rendered:
Welcome to a journey into the darker realms of construction where numbers can be as towering as the structures they’re meant to build. Today, we’re diving into a curious case from sunny Arizona, where a seemingly straightforward home construction has twisted into a labyrinth of lumber and numbers. This project gives us an excellent opportunity to show how advanced technology brings clarity to the construction process.

The Case at Hand

Craydl was brought on board to conduct a thorough audit for a grand 12,000-square-foot residence, an architectural dream begun in 2021 with its framing rising in 2022. Fast forward to 2024, and the frame stands largely complete. Here’s the kicker: the lumber, initially estimated to cost around $106,000, has skyrocketed in expense, culminating in a jaw-dropping final bill exceeding $700,000—over six times the initial estimate! The homeowner suspects this hefty sum includes materials not intended for their project.

To create a complete picture, we consider the timeline of construction, the scale of the home, and the expected material flow. Larger homes invite more complexity, and complexity invites both opportunity and confusion. This moment is where the right tools can deliver clarity.

The Tech Detective: Scan-to-BIM

How can we verify these suspicions now that the structure is mostly up? This is where our hero, Super BIM (Building Information Modeling), enters the stage.

Step 1: Gather Existing Data

Our baseline assumption is that the builder has accurately recorded all materials purchased specifically for this project, a low bar of operational fortitude. The invoiced total should align with these records, minus any agreed-upon markups, say 10%. Therefore, for a $700,000 bill, we should find receipts totaling approximately $636,364 for materials directly allocated to this build.

Step 2: Deploy Cutting-Edge Technology

Utilzing Scan-to-BIM, a process involving precise LiDAR scans of the construction site while it’s still accessible. This approach not only captures the current state of the construction but serves as the foundation to create a highly detailed model in Revit.

Step 3: Model and Analyze

In Revit (software developed by Autodesk), we employ best practices to model every aspect of the construction—from the framing to the roof and shear plans. This includes a meticulous setup of material assemblies and family utilization, ensuring every piece of lumber and hardware is accounted for in our digital blueprint.

Step 4: Material Schedule vs. Purchase Records

Once our model is complete, it generates a comprehensive material schedule that we then cross-reference with the list of materials billed to the customer. This not only confirms the accuracy of the billing but also highlights any discrepancies.

The Power of Super BIM

By harnessing the capabilities of BIM technology, we’re not just building structures; we’re building transparency and trust. This case shows how advanced tech like Scan-to-BIM can turn the tides in dispute resolution, ensuring every beam and nail is exactly where it should be—both physically and financially.

Now that’s not just cool; it’s Super BIM cool!

excerpt.rendered: Welcome to a journey into the darker realms of construction where numbers can be as towering as the structures they’re meant to build. Today, we’re diving into a curious case from sunny Arizona, where a seemingly straightforward home construction has twisted into a labyrinth of lumber and numbers. This project gives us an excellent opportunity […]

title.rendered: Revolutionizing Preconstruction: The Visionary Founder of Craydl

content.rendered:
In the world of homebuilding and remodeling, preconstruction processes and profitability often weigh heavily on professionals. Adam Katz, the driving force behind Craydl, has transformed the industry with his groundbreaking platform and services for preconstruction management.

Adam’s unique perspective combines financial acumen and hands-on construction experience gained during his 12-year stint on Wall Street and his passion for home redevelopment. He later transitioned to IT consulting and software development, becoming VP of Operations at ImageTag and streamlining complex processes, a skill that became central to Craydl’s mission.

Bridging the Gap in Construction Management

Craydl was created to address the lack of a unified platform for information management and services to support the preconstruction process. Adam drew on his experience in development, building, and IT consulting to develop a solution that streamlines communication and collaboration within the industry. The platform revolves around a “single version of the truth,” providing a centralized repository for all construction documentation and bidding processes.

Passionate Problem Solving and Impact

Adam’s passion for problem-solving led him to create meaningful change in the construction industry by building a team of like-minded professionals dedicated to dismantling dysfunctional construction processes.

With a deep-rooted commitment to drive change and Adam’s leadership, the Cradyl team has delivered tangible improvements, including a 10% margin gain on each construction project. Builders, architects, and other stakeholders within the construction “village” benefit from better communication, enhanced execution, margin optimization, and increased happiness.

Life Beyond Craydl: Family, Passion, and Learning

Adam Katz and his family

Adam is a devoted husband and father who loves that next challenge. When he’s not working or hanging out with his family, he can often be found on the ice, gliding effortlessly across the rink as he plays hockey with his friends. He also has a deep appreciation for the benefits of yoga and can often be found striking a pose in a heated studio.

Adam’s pursuit of self-improvement extends to his musical interests as well. Learning the guitar and mastering chords is a testament to his enduring curiosity and zest for learning. As he looks to the future, Adam envisions a symphony of success that harmonizes his passions with his unwavering dedication to revolutionizing the construction industry.

The Future of Preconstruction

Please browse the Craydl website to gain a more in-depth understanding of Adam’s vision and the work achieved by the Craydl team. Explore how Craydl’s innovative platform revolutionizes preconstruction management.
